query: features of reversible and irreversible cell injury

retrieved_docs.title: Cell Liquefactive Necrosis -- Introduction -- Reversible Cell Injury
retrieved_docs.content: In the early or mild stages of injury, cellular damage remains reversible if the stressor is removed. Although there may be noticeable structural and functional impairments during this phase, the cell retains the capacity to recover and restore normal function. This stage is characterized by alterations in metabolic pathways and organelles without permanent loss of cell viability. [4] If the harmful stimulus persists, the damage may progress beyond the point of repair, resulting in irreversible injury and ultimately cell death. These stages are central to understanding the cellular responses to various pathological conditions. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell Death -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Necrosis typically occurs when there is extensive damage to the cell membrane and internal structures that push the cell past reversible injury. It is usually the result of acute external factors, such as trauma, ischemia, toxins, or other non-physiologic mechanisms. Necrotic cell death results in a specific presentation such as damage to the plasma membrane, cellular swelling, and unregulated nuclear degradation.

retrieved_docs.title: Cell Liquefactive Necrosis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cells maintain their structure and function by adapting to environmental changes. [1] However, when subjected to severe stress, exposure to harmful agents, or intrinsic defects, cells may reach a threshold where adaptation is no longer possible, leading to cell injury. [2] This process involves various cellular and metabolic disruptions and can follow a trajectory from reversible injury to irreversible damage and cell death. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell Death -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The cellular characteristics of necrotic cell death do not always follow the pattern seen in ischemic injury, but it serves as a good example. Necrosis results in cell lysis and exposure of cytoplasmic contents in an uncontrolled setting as the plasma membrane is damaged, regardless of the type of damage that led it to this stage. A release of cellular content into the surrounding area generally elicits an inflammatory response not seen in apoptosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell Death -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Ischemia, for example, typically ends in necrotic cell death. With the decreased delivery of oxygen to the cell, oxidative phosphorylation performed by the mitochondria becomes compromised. This results in a decreased amount of ATP available in the cytoplasm. In cells with a Na+/K+ (sodium-potassium) pump, lack of sufficient ATP disables the transporter. Sodium begins to increase within the cell as it can no longer be pumped out. This results in cellular swelling as the increase in intracellular sodium pulls water in. Cellular swelling, blebbing, and loss of microvilli are typical presentations of necrotic cell death. If ATP is restored in time, these presentations are reversible. If ATP is not restored before further cell damage, the loss of ATP progresses to have extensive effects on the cell.

retrieved_docs.title: Cell Liquefactive Necrosis -- Introduction -- Coagulative Necrosis
retrieved_docs.content: This is the default pattern of necrosis associated with ischemia or hypoxia in every organ except the brain. [8] [12] Gross Appearance: Tissue is firm, and architecture is maintained for days after cell death Microscopic: Preserved cell outlines without nuclei

retrieved_docs.title: Central Toxic Keratopathy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The loss of stromal tissue is likely due to one of two possible mechanisms; either there could be apoptosis of keratocytes, enzymatic degradation of the stromal matrix, or a combination of the two. A lack of associated inflammation has made keratocyte death by apoptosis the predominant theory, and the striae and opacity that characterize the disease may be the gross visualization of this microscopic process. [8] [9] [12] [23] [24] Apoptosis leads to the loss of tissue, causing a corneal flattening that is predominantly found in the anterior tangential curvature of the cornea, while the posterior tangential curvature shows minimal changes. This change indicates that as the stromal cells undergo apoptosis, it is the anterior surface that may fall to fill the gap left behind. [23] Following the initial stromal loss, the steepening corrective phase occurs over subsequent weeks to months and is most active shortly after the thinning resolves. [23] This thickening process is less studied and may occur as part of epithelial wound healing and stromal remodeling. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) imaging demonstrating increased reflectivity may indicate that there may be a disorganized cellular matrix associated with this process. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Renal Tubular Necrosis -- Pathophysiology -- Later injury
retrieved_docs.content: Necroinflammation is another process by which ATN can occur. In necroinflammation, necrotic cells release DAMPs and expose cell antigens to cytotoxic T-cells, mediating an adaptive immune response and inflammation. Therefore, the necrotic death of tubular cells reduces kidney function, and the inflammation results in further damage. Necroptosis may cause fibroblast proliferation and irreversible fibrosis. [26] Most models favor ferroptosis as the most dominant process in ATN, which may partially explain the reversibility or improvement of most ATN cases. [26] [29] [30] Klotho is a transmembrane protein that functions as an obligate co-receptor for fibroblast growth factor 23; this may be renoprotective, and lower expression is associated with a higher degree of ATN. [31] Klotho is thought to reduce oxidative stress and is found to be low in both ischemic and nephrotoxic forms of ATN. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Shock -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The underlying molecular mechanisms for spinal shock remain largely unknown. Animal models have been used to study the pathophysiology of spinal shock, which elucidates the natural progression of injury in SCIs to some degree. After initial hemorrhagic foci in the gray matter, there appears to be significant protein accumulation in the gray matter of the spinal cord. Edema then ensues and peaks 3 to 6 days after the initial injury and can be visualized on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) for up to 2 weeks. The slow process of central cord necrosis and vacuolization subsequently occurs, continuing for about 2 months. The characteristically thin rim of white matter surrounding the central core of necrosis remains intact throughout this process. Often, patients with SCIs begin losing neurologic function above the level of the lesion several days after the initial insult, primarily due to spinal cord pathway rearrangement. Once this process abates, normal function above the injury returns, although the exact time needed for this process has not been defined and may last from weeks to months. If a patient survives the initial injury but remains immobile, the area fills with gliotic tissue. [13] The transection of the spinal cord results in spinal shock and transient extinction of reflexes below the level of injury. The main pathophysiological mechanisms include: [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Renal Tubular Necrosis -- Pathophysiology -- Later injury
retrieved_docs.content: The extension phase consists of 2 major events: Ongoing hypoxia following the ischemic event An inflammatory response

retrieved_docs.title: Inhalation Injury -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The histopathology of inhalation parenchymal injury is diffuse alveolar damage (DAD): diffuse edema with epithelial necrosis and cell sloughing. After this initial phase, hyaline membrane formation occurs. Finally, DAD may organize, leading to the proliferation of type II pneumocytes with resorption of the hyaline membranes and exudates, and fibroblast proliferation. The long-term effects of DAD include both complete recovery and permanent interstitial fibrosis. Many inhaled chemical toxins cause DAD.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Renal Tubular Necrosis -- Pathophysiology -- Recovery
retrieved_docs.content: The recovery phase is established by cellular repair, migration, and proliferation to maintain cellular and tubule integrity. The cellular function improves slowly as the cells repair and reorganize. Although the toll-like rececptors and macrophages mediate initial necroinflammation, once the inflammatory phase subsides a subset of macrophages, toll-like receptors, and remaining tubular epithelial cells secrete cytokines which help modulate epithelial cell repair. [24] [25] The blood flow returns to the normal range, and the cells establish intracellular homeostasis. As noted above, since ferroptosis is thought to be the dominant process and does not involve fibroblasts, regeneration of tubular cells is possible.

retrieved_docs.title: Cell Liquefactive Necrosis -- Introduction -- Cell Necrosis
retrieved_docs.content: When cells die by necrosis, they exhibit 2 major types of microscopes or macroscopic appearance. The first is liquefactive necrosis, also known as colliquative necrosis, which is characterized by partial or complete dissolution of dead tissue and transformation into a liquid, viscous mass. The loss of tissue and cellular profile occurs within hours in liquefactive necrosis. In contrast to liquefactive necrosis, coagulative necrosis, the other major pattern, is characterized by maintaining normal architecture of necrotic tissue for several days after cell death. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Traumatic Encephalopathy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Immuno-excitotoxicity is critical in the pathogenesis of CTE. Repeatedly primed microglia, after a repetitive head injury, fail to transition from a neuro-destructive mode to a reparative mode. This failure exacerbates the inflammatory insult to the brain by providing a favorable milieu amidst glutamate toxicity. [10] Excitotoxicity also generates reactive oxygen and nitrogen species. [9] Repeated head injury causes axonal degeneration and microtubular disintegration, leading to tau oligomerization. This oligomerization progresses to the formation of NFTs, affecting neuronal crosstalk and networking. This disruption eventually results in tau propagation, which triggers inflammatory cascades and ultimately impairs blood-brain barrier permeability. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell Death -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: If a cell is unable to adapt to increased stress, injury results. Cell injury is reversible until a certain threshold where it progresses to cell death. Historically, cell death has been designated into two classes: necrosis and apoptosis. Necrosis is often coined as accidental death as it is generally seen as not controlled by the cell. Apoptosis, on the other hand, is typically viewed as programmed cell death, regulated and controlled. The two differ in presentation, morphology, and mechanism though there has been recognized overlap, which will be discussed at the end of this review.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Trauma -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple complex pathways activate in response to damaged DNA, cellular stress, or infections. Here, we focus on the cellular response to mechanical trauma. Although there may be many mechanisms of traumatic injury to cells (physical damage or extreme temperature changes), the ultimate consequence is a loss of cellular integrity and function, eventually leading to cell death. Necroptosis is 1 mechanism in which cell death due to an injury can occur and is the most studied. The pathway begins with TNF alpha binding to its receptor, leading to the "RIPotosome" formation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 207
retrieved_docs.content: are organized within injury classification frameworks, share common external cause codes, and often involve similar pathological outcomes such as tissue damage and neurotoxicity, providing a cohesive framework for assessment and management of traumatic patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 867
retrieved_docs.content: Internal injury of chest typically results from a traumatic event and is characterized by tissue damage (تَلَف الأنسجة). This tissue damage may be accompanied by neurotoxic changes (سمية عصبية). An injury described as “injury undetermined whether accidentally or purposely inflicted, by crashing of motor vehicle” is classified as a Motor vehicle traffic (MVT) injury and shares the same associated morphology of tissue damage (تَلَف الأنسجة). Both the general internal injury of chest and the motor‑vehicle‑related injury are linked through the common pathological finding of tissue damage, which can involve neurotoxicity, underscoring the need to evaluate the traumatic mechanism and potential toxic effects in patient assessment and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2200
retrieved_docs.content: Neurotoxicity (سمية عصبية) is closely linked with tissue damage (تَلَف الأنسجة), which represents the characteristic morphological change seen in neurotoxic injury. The presence of tissue damage serves as a key indicator of neurotoxic effects on the nervous system, aiding clinicians in diagnosing, monitoring, and managing conditions where neurotoxic agents cause structural injury to tissues.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 833
retrieved_docs.content: generic unspecified abnormal‑finding categories, all of which fall within the larger umbrella of injuries and conditions arising from external causes, including toxic exposures such as heavy metals and physical mechanisms such as asphyxiation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2559
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “Due to” is associated with crushing injury or internal injury, indicating that these types of injuries are frequently described as occurring because of specific causative events. This association highlights the importance of identifying the underlying mechanisms when evaluating patients with crushing or internal injuries, as the wording “due to” often signals a direct link between the injury and its precipitating factor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2194
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of cervical spine with cord lesion is linked to تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage), and this tissue damage is further associated with سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity). Consequently, a cervical spine fracture that involves the spinal cord can lead to direct tissue injury that may produce neurotoxic effects, highlighting the importance of addressing both structural damage and its potential neurotoxic consequences in patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2088
retrieved_docs.content: The condition described as “Injury undetermined whether accidentally or purposely inflicted, by crashing of aircraft” is linked to the morphological finding of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage). In turn, the tissue damage (تَلَف الأنسجة) is associated with سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity). This relationship indicates that injuries resulting from an aircraft crash, even when the intent is unclear, may involve underlying tissue damage that can give rise to neurotoxic effects, highlighting the importance of evaluating both structural and neurotoxic consequences in the clinical assessment and management of such trauma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2562
retrieved_docs.content: Injury due to legal intervention by firearm and injury undetermined whether accidentally or purposely inflicted by crashing of aircraft are both linked to the morphological finding of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage). The presence of تَلَف الأنسجة is itself associated with سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity), indicating that such traumatic events may involve neurotoxic processes alongside structural tissue injury. Recognizing the connection between these mechanisms can inform clinical assessment and management of patients with firearm‑related legal interventions or aircraft‑related trauma.

retrieved_docs.content: grouped under a unified late‑effect classification that is further subdivided by anatomical region. This structured relationship aids clinicians in linking acute injury diagnoses with their potential long‑term sequelae and facilitates comprehensive patient management across different injury types and body regions.

retrieved_docs.content: to سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity) through a has_associated_morphology relationship, suggesting that tissue damage in these injuries may involve neurotoxic processes. Overall, the network of relationships connects firearm‑related external cause codes, specific injury classifications, anatomical injury hierarchies, co‑occurring fracture patterns, and underlying pathological morphologies such as tissue damage and neurotoxicity, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding how diverse mechanisms of trauma converge on similar clinical and pathological outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2027
retrieved_docs.content: Open wounds (جرح مفتوح) are clinically associated with crushing injuries or internal injuries, indicating that such traumatic mechanisms often result in an open wound presentation. Within the category of open wounds, cut wounds (جرح قطع) represent a specific subtype, meaning that a cut wound is a particular form of open wound. Recognizing that crushing or internal injuries can lead to open wounds, and that cut wounds are a distinct subset of these, aids clinicians in diagnosing the nature of the trauma and selecting appropriate management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 157
retrieved_docs.content: emphasizing its role as a specific cause of cerebral edema. Finally, إصابة حادة في الدماغ (acute brain injury) is considered a form of اعتلال دماغي, reinforcing the concept that acute traumatic or ischemic insults to the mozek constitute a type of brain disease. Together, these relationships illustrate how various imaging findings, inflammatory, metabolic, vascular, toxic, and traumatic processes converge on the mozek, linking structural abnormalities, physiological functions, and clinical syndromes within an integrated framework of brain health and disease.

Together, these relationships map a comprehensive hierarchy of general to specific injuries, illustrating how broad injury categories subdivide into distinct anatomical and pathological entities that are relevant for diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 266
retrieved_docs.content: hemorrhages ranging from focal (نزيف مشيمي) to extensive (نزيف مشيمي باثق), and various forms of choroidal detachment (serous, non‑hemorrhagic and hemorrhagic). Systemic diseases such as diabetes mellitus and hypertension produce retinal microvascular complications that frequently trigger proliferative neovascularization and choroidal‑retinal neovascularization with associated hemorrhage. Trauma and surgery contribute to post‑traumatic chorioretinal and choroidal scars, choroidal tears and choroidal detachment. Neoplastic spread may manifest as metastatic retinitis‑retinochoroiditis. Collectively, these entities demonstrate that inflammation, vascular occlusion, ischemia, metabolic disease, trauma, surgical injury, hereditary factors and neoplastic infiltration can each damage the choroid and retina, leading to scarring, atrophy, detachment, neovascularization and visual impairment. Accurate identification of the specific choroidal and retinal pathology is essential for comprehensive
